planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring before new growth emerges, or in fall after leaves drop. Space 18-24 inches apart to allow colonies to fill in over 2-3 seasons.

directions: Yellowroot is a superb native eastern North American shrub for challenging shaded sites, spreading slowly by rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing colonies. Its finely divided compound leaves emerge green in spring and turn a stunning bronze-orange-red in autumn, providing multi-season interest beneath deciduous trees. The bright yellow roots and inner bark are rich in berberine and were historically used as a natural dye. Plant divisions in moist, well-drained acidic soil in shade to part shade, and water regularly during the first season until established.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: All Acorus calamus in North America is triploid and sterile and does not set viable seed — propagate exclusively by rhizome division in early spring. Plant at or just below the waterline in boggy soil or shallow water up to 4 inches deep.

directions: Sweet Flag is a grass-like aromatic perennial grown for its bright green, sword-shaped leaves that emit a distinctive sweet, spicy fragrance when bruised or crushed. It thrives at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in boggy soils where it tolerates partial shade admirably. Because all North American populations of Acorus calamus are triploid and sterile, the plant reproduces solely by rhizome — divide clumps every 3-4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and spread. Plant rhizomes horizontally just below the soil surface at the water's edge or in shallow standing water.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in spring in moist, well-drained soil in a shaded to partially shaded location. Once established, Corydalis lutea self-seeds prolifically and will colonize crevices in walls, steps, and rocky slopes on its own.

directions: Yellow Corydalis is one of the longest-blooming shade perennials available, producing a nearly continuous flush of small golden-yellow tubular flowers from late spring through the first hard frosts of autumn. Its delicate, blue-green ferny foliage forms tidy mounds and pairs beautifully with hostas, ferns, and astilbes in woodland gardens. This European native excels at seeding itself into shaded crevices between stone walls, paving, and rocky slopes where few other plants will grow. Deadhead lightly to moderate self-seeding, or allow it to naturalize freely as a low-maintenance filler throughout shaded beds and borders.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as new growth begins, replanting immediately in cool, moist, humus-rich soil in deep shade. Handle rhizomes gently as they are slow to re-establish after disturbance.

directions: Clasping Twistedstalk is a graceful native woodland perennial notable for its distinctively zigzagging stems and oval clasping leaves that create an architectural layered effect in the shade garden. Small white to pale pink bell-shaped flowers hang beneath the leaves on twisted pedicels in late spring, giving way to showy red berries in late summer and fall that are relished by thrushes and other forest birds. It thrives in cool, moist, humus-rich soil under a canopy of deciduous trees and performs best where summers are cool. Amend planting areas generously with leaf mold or composted bark and maintain even moisture throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or early fall and replant at the same depth in well-drained to moist woodland soil in partial to full shade. Space 12-15 inches apart to create a weed-suppressing groundcover carpet within 2-3 growing seasons.

directions: Blue Wood Sedge is a refined native sedge with distinctive blue-green arching foliage that forms slowly spreading colonies, making it an outstanding low-maintenance groundcover for shaded sites. It is notably tolerant of dry shade once established — one of the most challenging garden conditions — and thrives beneath the root competition of mature oaks, maples, and other large deciduous trees. The fine-textured foliage contrasts beautifully with broad-leaved shade plants such as hostas, wild ginger, and trilliums in naturalistic woodland plantings. Mow or cut back to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges to keep plantings looking fresh.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in late summer to fall after bloom. Plant rhizomes horizontally just below the soil surface at pond margins or in shallow standing water up to 6 inches deep, or in consistently boggy soil. Check local regulations before planting — this species is considered invasive in many US states.

directions: Yellow Flag Iris is a robust and spectacular wetland iris bearing brilliant golden-yellow flowers in May and June atop bold, upright sword-shaped foliage reaching 3-5 feet tall. It excels at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in naturalized wetland plantings where it tolerates partial shade and stands in shallow standing water. However, gardeners should be aware that Iris pseudacorus is classified as an invasive species in many US states and Canadian provinces due to its aggressive spread by both rhizomes and prolific seed production in natural wetlands — check your state or local regulations before planting and deadhead spent flowers diligently to prevent seed dispersal. Where it can be grown responsibly, it is unmatched for bold color and structural presence in wet, shaded garden settings.
sources: USDA Forest Service Invasive Species Program
